title: How Stripe Calculates MRR (And Where the Math Breaks)
meta_description: Stripe's MRR number is wrong more often than you think. Here's exactly how it's calculated, where the logic breaks, and what to trust instead.
h1: How Stripe Calculates MRR, and Where Its Logic Quietly Breaks Down
body_markdown:
The first time I noticed Stripe's MRR number was lying to me was a Wednesday in March 2022. My Stripe dashboard said $8,400 MRR. My spreadsheet said $7,615. I spent four hours trying to figure out who was wrong, assumed it was me, and shipped a pricing page change based on Stripe's number. Two months later I realized Stripe had been counting a $780 annual plan as if it were a monthly plan for the first billing cycle, then correcting itself silently.

That was the moment I stopped trusting the MRR number in Stripe's Billing dashboard without understanding how it got there.

If you're running a bootstrapped SaaS on Stripe — somewhere between $1k and $50k MRR, probably checking the dashboard more than you'd admit — you need to know how Stripe calculates MRR and the specific places its logic quietly falls apart. Because it does. Regularly.

## The formula Stripe actually uses

Stripe's MRR calculation is documented, but you have to piece it together from a few different support articles and the Sigma schema. Here's the short version.

Stripe takes every active subscription, normalizes its billing interval to a monthly figure, and sums the result. A $29/month plan contributes $29. A $290/year plan contributes $24.17 ($290 ÷ 12). A $58 plan billed every two months contributes $29. Quarterly, semi-annual, weekly — all normalized to a monthly equivalent.

Then it subtracts coupon discounts (prorated to monthly) and excludes subscriptions in `trialing`, `incomplete`, `incomplete_expired`, `canceled`, and `unpaid` states. Only `active` and `past_due` count.

Simple enough. Until it isn't.

The breakdown starts with a question Stripe never really answers cleanly: *what counts as a subscription?* Because Stripe has at least four ways to charge a customer recurringly, and its MRR logic only really understands one of them well.

## Where the logic breaks

Here are the specific failure modes I've hit, roughly in order of how often they bite founders I talk to.

**Annual plans in the month they're sold.** This is the big one. When someone pays you $1,200 for an annual plan, Stripe correctly adds $100 to your MRR. But your *cash* that month is $1,200. If you're mentally equating "Stripe MRR × 12" with "approximate ARR", fine. If you're equating MRR growth with revenue growth — especially when you're running a Black Friday annual deal — you're going to make bad decisions. I did. I ran a 30% off annual promo in November 2022, saw MRR barely move, panicked, and almost killed the promo early. It turned out to be the best revenue month of the year. The MRR number was technically correct and practically misleading.

**One-time charges for recurring-ish things.** Stripe only counts `Subscription` objects. If you charge setup fees, usage overages as one-time invoices, or — god forbid — you built your own recurring billing on top of `PaymentIntents` because you wanted custom logic, none of it shows up in MRR. I know three founders doing this. One of them has roughly $4k/month in "recurring" revenue that doesn't exist to Stripe at all.

**Usage-based billing.** If you use metered billing, Stripe computes MRR based on the subscription's base price, not actual usage. A customer on a $0 base plan who racks up $800/month in usage contributes $0 to MRR. OpenAI's entire API business would register as $0 MRR in Stripe if you ran it this way. This got so bad that Stripe shipped a separate revenue reporting view in 2023 that handles usage differently, but the default MRR chart in Billing still doesn't.

**Pauses and custom billing cycles.** Stripe added subscription pause behavior in 2020 and subscription schedules got a real overhaul around the same time. If you pause a sub with `behavior: keep_as_draft`, Stripe sometimes keeps it in MRR and sometimes doesn't, depending on the exact state. I spent a weekend in 2023 figuring out that paused subscriptions were inflating my MRR by about $600 because the status was still `active` but no invoice would ever generate.

**Discounts that expire.** Coupons get prorated into MRR, which seems right, but if you have a 50%-off-for-3-months coupon, MRR shows the discounted amount for three months and then jumps when the discount expires. So your "MRR growth" in month four includes a phantom bump from expiring discounts that has nothing to do with new customers or expansion. This one took me embarrassingly long to notice.

**Failed payments and dunning.** A subscription in `past_due` still counts toward MRR. Which is arguably correct — the customer hasn't churned yet — but it means your MRR number includes customers who are about to churn involuntarily. For a SaaS at 3% involuntary churn (pretty normal) this is maybe 1-2% of MRR that's functionally already gone.

**Proration.** When a customer upgrades mid-cycle, Stripe creates proration line items. The MRR adjusts immediately to the new plan price. Good. But if you look at an MRR chart with daily granularity, you'll see weird single-day spikes and dips from proration invoices that make trend analysis harder than it needs to be.

## The Baremetrics question

So if Stripe's MRR is unreliable, what do people use?

For years the answer was Baremetrics. I paid them $129/month for the Startup plan from late 2021 through mid-2023. It was fine. Their MRR calculation is more opinionated than Stripe's — they classify movements into new, expansion, contraction, reactivation, and churn, which Stripe's native dashboard still doesn't do cleanly. ChartMogul is the other obvious option and honestly I think their numbers are slightly more accurate, though the UI is busier.

Here's my opinion, and I'll commit to it: if you're under $20k MRR, paying $129/month for Baremetrics is hard to justify. That's almost 1% of your revenue going to a dashboard. At $50k MRR, sure, it's rounding error. At $8k MRR it's a line item I'd cut. Both Baremetrics and ChartMogul are priced for companies that have already made it, not companies trying to.

But here's the honest caveat — and this is the part where I got it wrong for six months — just because you can't justify $129/month doesn't mean the Stripe default is good enough. It isn't. The gap between "Stripe dashboard" and "Baremetrics" is where most sub-$20k SaaS founders live, and it's a pretty lonely gap.

## What to actually do

A few things that have worked for me and for other small-scale founders I've compared notes with:

1. Separate your MRR view from your cash view. Keep both. Look at both weekly. MRR tells you about health; cash tells you whether you can pay rent.
2. Pull your own numbers from the Stripe API at least once. Even a Google Sheet with a one-time export will show you things the dashboard hides — particularly around annual plans, failed payments, and the actual state distribution of your subscriptions.
3. Decide, explicitly, how you want to treat past-due subscriptions. Include them? Exclude them after 7 days? After 14? Stripe's default is to include them for the dunning window (usually 3-4 weeks depending on your settings) and that's probably too generous.
4. Stop refreshing the dashboard. Seriously. I used to check Stripe maybe 20 times a day and it was one of the worst habits I've ever had as a founder. The number doesn't move faster because you watch it.

On that last point.

I built [Stripe Revenue Tracker](https://example.com) partly because I was tired of logging into Stripe all day just to see a number that was subtly wrong anyway. It normalizes the stuff I talked about above — annual plans, past-due logic, expiring discounts — and sends you the number once a day instead of making you go get it. If that's useful, great. If you'd rather write a Sigma query and pipe it to a Slack webhook, that also works and costs nothing.

The deeper point isn't which tool you use. It's that the MRR number on your Stripe dashboard is a model, not a measurement, and the model makes assumptions that stop being true the moment your billing gets even slightly interesting. Annual plans, usage billing, discounts, pauses, dunning — each one is a place where Stripe had to pick a default, and their defaults were picked for a median customer who probably doesn't look like you.

Once you know where the math bends, you can decide which bends matter for your business and which ones don't. For me it was annual plans and expiring discounts. For a usage-billed API company it'll be something completely different. For someone running a $9/month consumer subscription with no annual option and no coupons, honestly, the Stripe dashboard is probably fine.

The worst version of this is the one I was in for most of 2022 — trusting a number I didn't understand, making product decisions off of it, and then getting quietly surprised every few months when reality didn't match the dashboard. That's the trap. Not that Stripe is wrong. That you think it's right.

A fractured geometric line chart breaking apart into mismatched segments, some segments floating loose, others overlapping at wrong angles. Thin vector linework, subtle grid texture, suggestion of depth through layered translucent planes.
